topic: Two coworkers are stuck in an elevator for three hours
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the metal box was a physical weight pressing down on Clara. Every minute stretched into an agonizing expanse of nothingness. Her palms were slick against the cool, brushed steel of the elevator doors, and she kept flexing her fingers, a nervous habit she couldn't seem to break. She watched the faint, sickly yellow light of the emergency strip flicker rhythmically, each flash sending a jolt of adrenaline through her system. *How long?* The thought hammered against her skull. She shifted her weight from one foot to the other, the cheap fabric of her slacks suddenly feeling abrasive against her skin. She tried to focus on the small details—the faint smell of ozone, the almost imperceptible hum of the machinery—anything to distract from the suffocating awareness of the trapped situation. A shallow breath hitched in her chest, and she squeezed her eyes shut, willing the time to move, to break, to end.

[story 2]

"This is not funny," Mark muttered, his voice tight and strained. He kept running a hand repeatedly through his already disheveled hair, his eyes darting frantically between the indicator panel and Clara. His jaw was clenched so hard she could see the tension radiating from his neck. He began tapping a rapid, uneven rhythm on the side of the elevator car, a frantic attempt to impose some order on the chaos swirling inside him. *Three hours. Three hours staring at this box.* A cold knot tightened in his stomach, making his breathing shallow and quick. He began counting the rivets on the wall, a tedious, pointless exercise designed to keep his mind from spiraling into worst-case scenarios. Every creak of the building above sounded like a threat, a sign that something was about to give way.

[story 3]

I kept checking my phone, though the screen offered only a mocking expanse of dead bars. The fluorescent lighting above buzzed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly inside my sinuses. My hands were clasped so tightly together that my knuckles were white, and I found myself tracing the seam where the door met the frame over and over again. Each repetition felt like a desperate plea for the situation to resolve itself, to change state. A bead of sweat traced a slow, cold path down my temple. I forced myself to look at the reflection in the polished metal—a distorted, pale version of myself staring back, wide-eyed and utterly still. The waiting was excruciating; my muscles felt wound tight, ready to snap from the sheer, prolonged stillness.
